message.content: fazer pesquisa profunda na web e nos arquivos em ./docs de modo a criar nos arquivos de planejamento do harness aqui uma parefa que implemente todas as melhores praticas em relacao a renderizacao de dados massivos em webgb e outros topicos relacionado, é de extrema importancia que haja uma task dedicada e pesquisa dedicada a encontrar a forma mais performatica para este projeto de modo que o canvas deixe de renderizar e/ou calcular renderizacao de coisas que não estão na viewport, com foco em performance. Deve ser feito planejamento profundo, cadastro das tasks no harness, revisao do plano e edicao se necessario e depois para e aguardar instrucoes.

content.thinking:
O usuário quer que eu faça uma pesquisa profunda sobre melhores práticas de renderização de dados massivos em WebGL, com foco especial em:

1. Viewport culling (não renderizar o que está fora da viewport)
2. Performance otimizada para WebGL
3. Práticas relacionadas a dados massivos

Ele quer que eu:
1. Pesquise na web e nos arquivos em ./docs
2. Crie tarefas no harness (features.json e claude-progress.txt)
3. Faça um planejamento profundo
4. Cadastre as tasks
5. Revise o plano
6. Pare e aguarde instruções

Vou começar lendo os arquivos relevantes e fazendo pesquisas na web.

content.text: Vou fazer uma pesquisa profunda sobre renderização massiva em WebGL e práticas de viewport culling. Deixe-me começar lendo os arquivos existentes e fazendo pesquisas na web.

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "0.3.1",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-17",
  "product": {
    "entry": "apps/canvas",
    "description": "Production React app with semantic zoom (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6)"
  },
  "reference": {
    "entry": "design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html",
    "description": "Standalone POC for visual reference only"
  },
  "priorityQueue": {
    "description": "Features ordered by execution priority. Execute in order, respecting blockedBy dependencies.",
    "note": "Priority 0 = immediate, Priority 1 = high, Priority 2 = after dependencies"
  },
  "parallelExecution": {
    "description": "Parallel execution configuration for remaining features (updated 2025-12-17)",
    "runner": "bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts",
    "worktreeDir": ".trees/",
    "status": "MOSTLY COMPLETE - Only 6 features remain",
    "waves": [
      {
        "wave": 1,
        "description": "Navigation enhancements + Task panel (can run in parallel)",
        "maxConcurrency": 3,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-A", "features": ["F016"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Shift+Scroll vertical pan"},
          {"id": "group-B", "features": ["F026"], "sequential": false, "files": ["App.tsx"], "note": "Task details panel"},
          {"id": "group-C", "features": ["F027", "F028"], "sequential": true, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Keyboard shortcuts zoom/pan"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 2,
        "description": "Accessibility (depends on Wave 1)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 1,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-D", "features": ["F021"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "layers"], "note": "Keyboard task navigation"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 3,
        "description": "Final accessibility (depends on F021)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 2,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-E", "features": ["F022"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "MicroLayer.tsx"], "note": "ARIA labels"}
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "features": [
    {
      "id": "STATE-ARCH-001",
      "category": "architecture",
      "priority": 1,
      "description": "Refatorar arquitetura de state management para padrão Dual-Store de alta performance",
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "research": "docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf",
      "rationale": "Documento de pesquisa identifica GC como gargalo principal em aplicações WebGL móveis. React Hooks e stores reativos são inadequados para updates a 60fps. Propõe arquitetura Dual-Store separando Simulation State (TypedArrays/ECS) de Application State (@mms/toolkit state-machine). NOTA: Usamos @mms/toolkit (packages/toolkit) para UI state, NUNCA Zustand.",
      "implementationPlan": {
        "phase1_foundation": {
          "description": "Criar infraestrutura base para Entity Store",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar EntityStore baseado em TypedArrays com padrão Structure of Arrays (SoA)",
            "Implementar Float32Array para posições (startDay, duration) e Uint8Array para flags (hasConflict, type)",
            "Criar Views/Cursors para acesso ergonômico aos dados tipados",
            "Benchmark: 100k entidades devem ocupar ~800KB vs dezenas de MB com objetos JS"
          ]
        },
        "phase2_spatial_index": {
          "description": "Implementar indexação espacial com R-tree",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar RBush para R-tree (npm i rbush)",
            "Criar wrapper para inserção/query de tasks por viewport bounds",
            "Otimizar: bulk loading em startup, rebuild apenas em create/delete",
            "Target: queries de viewport com 1000 resultados em <5ms"
          ]
        },
        "phase3_ui_bridge": {
          "description": "Criar bridge entre Engine State e UI via @mms/toolkit",
          "tasks": [
            "Usar @mms/toolkit state-machine existente (packages/toolkit) para estado de UI",
            "Criar slice 'entities' com selectedEntityId, hoveredEntityId para binding",
            "useSyncExternalStore já implementado no toolkit - updates síncronos garantidos",
            "Throttle de sincronização Engine→UI a 30fps via scheduler (não pelo store)",
            "NUNCA usar Zustand - @mms/toolkit é a solução oficial do projeto"
          ]
        },
        "phase4_object_pooling": {
          "description": "Implementar Object Pooling para vetores temporários",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar VectorPool para Vector3/Vector2 temporários no render loop",

     3→## Session: 2025-12-17 (STATE-ARCH-001 Planning) - COMPLETE ✅
     4→
     5→### STATE-ARCH-001: High-Performance State Management Architecture - PLANNED
     6→
     7→**Objective:**
     8→Plan refactoring of state management architecture based on research document analyzing Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS, and WebGL game engines.
     9→
    10→**Research Document:**
    11→- `docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf`
    12→- Key finding: GC (Garbage Collection) is the primary bottleneck, not rendering
    13→- React Hooks and reactive stores inadequate for 60fps on mobile
    14→- Recommends Dual-Store: Simulation State (TypedArrays) + Application State (external store)
    15→
    16→**Task Created: STATE-ARCH-001**
    17→
    18→Added comprehensive task to features.json with 5 implementation phases:
    19→
    20→1. **Phase 1 - Foundation:** Create EntityStore with TypedArrays (Structure of Arrays pattern)
    21→2. **Phase 2 - Spatial Index:** Implement RBush R-tree for viewport queries
    22→3. **Phase 3 - UI Bridge:** Connect Engine State to @mms/toolkit (NOT Zustand as PDF suggests)
    23→4. **Phase 4 - Object Pooling:** Eliminate GC pauses in animation loops
    24→5. **Phase 5 - Undo/Redo:** Command Pattern + Immer patches
    25→
    26→**CRITICAL DECISION: @mms/toolkit over Zustand**
    27→
    28→The PDF recommends Zustand for UI state, but this project uses **@mms/toolkit** (packages/toolkit):
    29→- Already implemented and integrated
    30→- Uses Immer for immutability
    31→- Uses useSyncExternalStore for synchronous updates
    32→- Has slice-based architecture matching project needs
    33→

    39→- 100,000+ entities renderable
    40→- 60fps constant during pan/zoom
    41→- <100MB memory for 100k entities
    42→- <5ms viewport queries
    43→- Zero perceptible GC pauses

    47→- Start with Phase 1 (EntityStore) when prioritized

   370→└── Group F: F021 (Keyboard task navigation) → Multiple files [HIGH risk]
   371→
   372→WAVE 4 (After Wave 3 merge - 1 group):
   373→└── Group G: F022 (ARIA labels) → Multiple files [LOW risk]
   374→```
   375→
   376→**Files Created:**
   377→- `scripts/parallel-config.ts` - Wave/group configuration with conflict analysis
   378→- `scripts/parallel-runner.ts` - Interactive parallel execution runner
   379→- `docs/PARALLEL_EXECUTION.md` - Complete documentation
   380→
   381→**Files Modified:**
   382→- `.gitignore` - Added `.trees/` for worktree directories
   383→- `features.json` - Added `parallelExecution` section with waves configuration
   384→
   385→**Key Features of Parallel Runner:**
   386→1. **Git worktree management** - Creates isolated working directories
   387→2. **Terminal session spawning** - Opens separate Claude sessions per feature
   388→3. **Isolation prompts** - Each session restricted to its feature scope
   389→4. **Wave dependencies** - Prevents starting Wave N before Wave N-1 completes
   390→5. **Merge helper** - Automates feature branch merging
   391→6. **Status display** - Shows progress across all waves/groups
   392→7. **macOS notifications** - Alerts for session events
   393→8. **Cleanup command** - Removes all worktrees
   394→
   395→**Speedup Calculation:**
   396→- Sequential: 10 features = 10 sessions
   397→- Parallel: 4 waves (2+2+1+1) = ~4 "effective sessions"
   398→- Estimated speedup: **2.5x**
   399→
   400→**Usage:**
   401→```bash
   402→# Show status
   403→bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts status
   404→
   405→# Execute Wave 1
   406→bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts wave 1
   407→
   408→# Merge completed feature
   409→bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts merge F023
   410→```

   562→## Session: 2025-12-17 (TECH-DEBT-001 Implementation) - COMPLETE ✅
   563→
   564→### TECH-DEBT-001: Migrate Camera State to @mms/toolkit State Machine - COMPLETE
   565→
   566→**Objective:**
   567→Replace React useState/useRef with @mms/toolkit external store to solve stale closure problems in animation loops.
   568→
   569→**Implementation:**
   570→
   571→1. **Created viewport slice (`apps/canvas/src/state/viewport.ts`):**
   572→   - ViewportSliceState interface with camera, canvasSize, momentum, animation, pan states
   573→   - createSyncSlice with all reducers: setCamera, setMomentum, startAnimation, startPan, etc.
   574→   - Cache policy: 'session' (resets when app closes)
   575→
   576→2. **Created canvas machine (`apps/canvas/src/state/canvas-machine.ts`):**
   577→   - createMachine with viewport slice
   578→   - Exports: useCanvas, useCanvasSelector, canvasActions, canvasStore
   579→   - Context with userID: null (anonymous canvas)
   580→
   581→3. **Migrated InfiniteCanvas.tsx:**
   582→   - Replaced useState calls with useCanvasSelector for reactive state
   583→   - Animation loops use canvasStore.getState() for synchronous access (no stale closures)
   584→   - Pan/zoom handlers use canvasStore.dispatch(canvasActions.viewport.xxx())
   585→   - Removed cameraPosRef workaround (no longer needed with external store)
   586→
   587→**Errors Fixed:**
   588→- `cache: { strategy: 'none' }` → `cache: { policy: 'session' }` (toolkit requires 'policy' field)
   589→- `context: {}` → `context: { userID: null }` (toolkit requires userID in context)
   590→
   591→**Files Created:**
   592→- `apps/canvas/src/state/viewport.ts` (~235 lines)
   593→- `apps/canvas/src/state/canvas-machine.ts` (~55 lines)
   594→- `apps/canvas/src/state/index.ts` (re-exports)
   595→
   596→**Files Modified:**
   597→- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (full migration from useState to store)

   601→- ✅ MESO layer (1.00x): Task blocks with row backgrounds
   602→- ✅ MICRO layer (2.25x): Task cards with labels
   603→- ✅ Zoom in/out buttons: Working (1.00x → 2.25x → 5.00x)
   604→- ✅ LOD transitions: Smooth (MESO → MICRO → back)
   605→- ✅ Reset View: Returns to initial state
   606→- ✅ No console errors
   607→
   608→**Benefits Achieved:**
   609→- Animation loops always read current state via store.getState()
   610→- No more stale closure issues in momentum/zoom animations
   611→- Clean separation of concerns (state logic in slice, rendering in component)

   771→### PERF-001: Performance Monitor with Logging System - COMPLETE
   772→
   773→**Objective:**
   774→Add real-time WebGL performance metrics tool with detailed logging for both visual and programmatic analysis.
   775→
   776→**Implementation:**
   777→
   778→1. **r3f-perf Integration:**

   783→   - ~400 lines of code with types, logging system, and visual panel
   784→   - PerfLogger sub-component tracks metrics every frame
   785→   - Circular buffer stores last 60s of performance data
   786→   - Rolling averages calculated over 60-frame windows
   787→
   788→3. **Global Console API:**
   789→   - `window.__PERF_LOG__` - Access log buffer (last 60s)
   790→   - `window.__PERF_EXPORT__()` - Export full log as array
   791→   - `window.__PERF_START_SESSION__(name)` - Start recording session
   792→   - `window.__PERF_STOP_SESSION__()` - Stop and get aggregated stats
   793→   - `window.__PERF_SESSION_STATUS__()` - Check active session
   794→   - `window.__PERF_CLEAR__()` - Clear log buffer
   795→
   796→4. **Session Statistics:**
   797→   - FPS: avg, min, max, p1 (worst 1%), p99 (best 99%)
   798→   - MS: avg, max frame time

   810→- Root cause: troika-three-utils 0.49.0 incompatible with Three.js 0.170+

   813→- Does NOT affect functionality - panel and logging work correctly

   952→### F116: Performance optimization (60fps) - COMPLETE

   959→
   960→1. **MesoLayer (BEFORE):**
   961→   - TaskBlocks: InstancedMesh ✅ (already optimized)

   966→   - Frustum culling (filterVisibleTasks)

   968→   - InstancedMesh for row backgrounds

   972→3. **MacroLayer:** Already optimized

   978→- **Before:** 50 individual `<mesh>` elements = 50 draw calls
   979→- **After:** 1 `<instancedMesh>` = 1 draw call
   980→- **Improvement:** 98% reduction in draw calls for row backgrounds

  1007→## Session: 2025-12-16 (F122 - Fix elastic/jump drag bug) - COMPLETE ✅
  1008→
  1009→### F122: Fix elastic/jump effect when dragging - COMPLETE
  1010→
  1011→**Problem:**
  1012→Canvas felt elastic or jumped when starting a new drag during/after momentum animation.
  1013→
  1014→**Root Cause Analysis:**
  1015→React state updates are batched/async. When user starts a new drag while momentum is running:
  1016→1. `handlePointerDown` captures `cameraPos` from React state
  1017→2. But React state hasn't been updated yet with latest momentum position
  1018→3. New drag starts from stale position → canvas "jumps" back
  1019→
  1020→**Solution Implemented:**
  1021→1. Added `cameraPosRef` to track camera position synchronously via useRef
  1022→2. Created `updateCameraPos()` helper that updates BOTH ref and state
  1023→3. Updated all `setCameraPos()` calls to use `updateCameraPos()`:
  1024→   - `handleCameraInitialized`
  1025→   - `animateZoomTo` (zoom animation loop)
  1026→   - `startMomentum` (momentum animation loop)
  1027→   - `handlePinchZoom`
  1028→   - `handlePointerMove` (pan movement)
  1029→   - `handleDoubleClick` (double-click zoom animation)
  1030→4. Changed `handlePointerDown` to read from `cameraPosRef.current` instead of `cameraPos` state
  1031→
  1032→**Files Changed:**
  1033→- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (cameraPosRef, updateCameraPos helper)
  1034→- `features.json` (F122 passes: true)
  1035→
  1036→**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
  1037→- ✅ Single drag pans correctly
  1038→- ✅ Drag during momentum - no jump/elastic effect
  1039→- ✅ Rapid drag-release-drag sequences (5 iterations) - smooth panning
  1040→- ✅ No console errors

  1060→## Session: 2025-12-16 (F120 + F121 - Double-click zoom + Scroll normalization) - COMPLETE ✅
  1061→
  1062→### F120: Double-click to zoom in - COMPLETE
  1063→
  1064→**Objective:**
  1065→Implement double-click to zoom in 2x centered on click location.
  1066→
  1067→**Implementation:**
  1068→- Already implemented in F119 (smooth zoom) session
  1069→- `handleDoubleClick` function in InfiniteCanvas.tsx (lines 1009-1074)
  1070→- Zooms 2x toward click point with 350ms cubicOut animation
  1071→- Respects MAX_ZOOM (5.0x) limit
  1072→
  1073→**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
  1074→- ✅ Double-click at 1.00x → 2.00x (2x zoom)
  1075→- ✅ Double-click at 2.00x → 4.00x (2x zoom)
  1076→- ✅ Double-click at 4.00x → 5.00x (clamped to MAX_ZOOM)
  1077→- ✅ Double-click at 5.00x stays at 5.00x (respects limit)
  1078→- ✅ Smooth animation with cubicOut easing
  1079→- ✅ No console errors
  1080→
  1081→### F121: Cross-platform scroll/input normalization - COMPLETE
  1082→
  1083→**Objective:**
  1084→Normalize scroll sensitivity across macOS/Windows/Linux and trackpad/mouse.
  1085→
  1086→**Implementation:**
  1087→
  1088→1. **deltaMode Normalization:**
  1089→   - LINE mode (deltaMode=1): multiply by LINE_HEIGHT (40px)
  1090→   - PAGE mode (deltaMode=2): multiply by PAGE_HEIGHT (800px)
  1091→   - PIXEL mode (deltaMode=0): use directly
  1092→
  1093→2. **Trackpad vs Mouse Detection:**
  1094→   - Track event frequency with timestamps
  1095→   - Events < 20ms apart = trackpad behavior
  1096→   - After 3+ rapid events, mark as trackpad
  1097→   - Large deltas (>50px) reset to mouse mode
  1098→
  1099→3. **Adaptive Sensitivity:**
  1100→   - Mouse: MOUSE_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.003
  1101→   - Trackpad: TRACKPAD_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001 (lower for many small events)
  1102→
  1103→4. **Zoom Delta Clamping:**
  1104→   - MAX_ZOOM_DELTA_PER_EVENT = 0.5
  1105→   - Prevents jump zoom from large scroll events
  1106→
  1107→**Constants Added:**
  1108→```typescript
  1109→LINE_HEIGHT = 40                    // Pixels per line
  1110→PAGE_HEIGHT = 800                   // Pixels per page
  1111→MOUSE_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.003      // Base zoom sensitivity
  1112→TRACKPAD_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001   // Lower for trackpad
  1113→MAX_ZOOM_DELTA_PER_EVENT = 0.5      // Cap per event
  1114→TRACKPAD_DETECTION_THRESHOLD = 20   // ms between events
  1115→TRACKPAD_EVENT_COUNT_THRESHOLD = 3  // Rapid events to confirm
  1116→```
  1117→
  1118→**Files Changed:**
  1119→- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (normalizeWheelDelta function, updated handleWheel)
  1120→- `features.json` (F120, F121 passes: true)
  1121→
  1122→**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
  1123→- ✅ Scroll down (deltaY=300) zooms out: 1.00x → 0.61x
  1124→- ✅ Scroll up (deltaY=-500) zooms in: 0.61x → 1.00x
  1125→- ✅ No console errors
  1126→- ✅ Smooth zoom animation
  1127→
  1128→**Next:** F116 (Performance), F117 (Visual parity)
  1129→
  1130→---
  1131→
  1132→## Session: 2025-12-16 (F118 - Momentum Panning) - COMPLETE ✅
  1133→
  1134→### F118: Momentum/Inertia Panning - COMPLETE
  1135→
  1136→**Objective:**
  1137→Implement velocity-based drag with momentum/inertia like Google Maps and iOS.
  1138→
  1139→**Implementation:**
  1140→
  1141→1. **Velocity Tracking:**
  1142→   - Track pointer position samples during drag with timestamps
  1143→   - Store last N samples (5) for velocity calculation
  1144→   - Calculate total displacement and time to get velocity
  1145→
  1146→2. **Momentum Animation:**
  1147→   - On pointer release, calculate average velocity from samples
  1148→   - Start `requestAnimationFrame` loop with initial velocity
  1149→   - Apply friction (0.93) per frame to gradually slow down
  1150→   - Stop when velocity < 0.5 world units
  1151→
  1152→3. **Integration:**
  1153→   - Momentum stops on any user interaction (new drag, zoom)
  1154→   - Cleanup animation on component unmount
  1155→   - Camera position clamped to world bounds during animation
  1156→
  1157→**Constants Used:**
  1158→```typescript
  1159→MOMENTUM_FRICTION = 0.93     // Per-frame velocity multiplier
  1160→MOMENTUM_MIN_VELOCITY = 0.5  // Stop threshold
  1161→MOMENTUM_VELOCITY_SAMPLES = 5 // Samples to average
  1162→```
  1163→
  1164→**Files Changed:**
  1165→- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (momentum implementation)
  1166→- `features.json` (F118 passes: true)
  1167→
  1168→**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
  1169→- ✅ App loads at Jan 2024 (initial position)
  1170→- ✅ Fast drag moves view with momentum continuation
  1171→- ✅ View moved from Jan 2024 to Dec 2024 with single fast drag
  1172→- ✅ Momentum respects world bounds
  1173→- ✅ No runtime errors related to momentum
  1174→
  1175→**Known Issue (pre-existing):**
  1176→
  1177→---
  1178→
  1179→## Historical Sessions Summary (Condensed)

# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Session: 2025-12-17 (STATE-ARCH-001 Planning) - COMPLETE ✅

### STATE-ARCH-001: High-Performance State Management Architecture - PLANNED

**Objective:**
Plan refactoring of state management architecture based on research document analyzing Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS, and WebGL game engines.

**Research Document:**
- `docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf`
- Key finding: GC (Garbage Collection) is the primary bottleneck, not rendering
- React Hooks and reactive stores inadequate for 60fps on mobile
- Recommends Dual-Store: Simulation State (TypedArrays) + Application State (external store)

**Task Created: STATE-ARCH-001**

Added comprehensive task to features.json with 5 implementation phases:

1. **Phase 1 - Foundation:** Create EntityStore with TypedArrays (Structure of Arrays pattern)
2. **Phase 2 - Spatial Index:** Implement RBush R-tree for viewport queries
3. **Phase 3 - UI Bridge:** Connect Engine State to @mms/toolkit (NOT Zustand as PDF suggests)
4. **Phase 4 - Object Pooling:** Eliminate GC pauses in animation loops
5. **Phase 5 - Undo/Redo:** Command Pattern + Immer patches

**CRITICAL DECISION: @mms/toolkit over Zustand**

The PDF recommends Zustand for UI state, but this project uses **@mms/toolkit** (packages/toolkit):
- Already implemented and integrated
- Uses Immer for immutability
- Uses useSyncExternalStore for synchronous updates
- Has slice-based architecture matching project needs

**Files Changed:**
- `features.json` - Added STATE-ARCH-001 task with detailed implementation plan
- `CLAUDE.md` - Added "High-Performance Architecture" section with guidelines

**Performance Targets:**
- 100,000+ entities renderable
- 60fps constant during pan/zoom
- <100MB memory for 100k entities
- <5ms viewport queries
- Zero perceptible GC pauses

**Next Steps:**
- Implementation will be phased over multiple sessions
- Start with Phase 1 (EntityStore) when prioritized

---

## Session: 2025-12-17 (UX Bug Fixes) - IN PROGRESS 🔄

### UX Bug Fix Sprint

**Context:**
User reported multiple UX bugs after features were marked complete. This session addresses all reported issues.

**Bugs Tracked in features.json:**
- BUG-001: Zoom freezes/stops working after interactions (PENDING)
- BUG-002: Sidebar (train list) doesn't have scroll (PENDING)
- BUG-003: Trackpad 2-finger scroll only zooms, should pan (PENDING)
- BUG-004: Minimap not like Google Maps style (PENDING)
- BUG-005: Date values not visible at certain zoom levels (PENDING)
- BUG-006: PerfMonitor blocks UI (FIXED ✅)
- BUG-007: Sidebar/train row alignment (PENDING)
- BUG-008: Elastic/momentum drag bug (FIXED ✅)

**Fixes Applied:**

1. **BUG-008 FIXED** - Disabled momentum panning in `InfiniteCanvas.tsx`:
   - Commented out `startMomentum()` call in `handlePointerUp`
   - Momentum was causing elastic/jumping behavior despite cameraPosRef fix
   - Can be reimplemented later with proper state synchronization

2. **BUG-006 FIXED** - Moved PerfMonitor from `top-right` to `bottom-left`:
   - Changed default position in `PerfMonitor.tsx`
   - Prevents blocking navbar controls (Reset View, Perf button)

**Harness State Saved:**
- All bugs added to features.json with priority 0
- BUG-006, BUG-008 marked as passes: true
- Remaining bugs have passes: false

**Pending Fixes (Priority Order):**
1. BUG-003: Add 2-finger pan support (without modifiers)
2. BUG-002: Fix sidebar scroll (overflow-y)
3. BUG-007: Fix sidebar/train row alignment
4. BUG-004: Refactor minimap to Google Maps style (no X, always visible)
5. BUG-005: Fix date visibility at extreme zoom
6. BUG-001: Test zoom after other fixes

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (disabled momentum)
- `apps/canvas/src/components/PerfMonitor.tsx` (moved to bottom-left)
- `features.json` (added BUG-001 through BUG-008)

**Dev Server:** http://localhost:5177

---

## Session: 2025-12-17 (F022 - ARIA Labels for Screen Readers) - COMPLETE ✅

### F022: ARIA labels for screen readers - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Add ARIA attributes and live region announcements to make the canvas accessible to screen readers.

**Implementation:**

1. **Added ARIA attributes to canvas container (InfiniteCanvas.tsx):**
   - `role="application"` - indicates interactive canvas
   - `aria-label` - full context description with LOD level, zoom percentage, navigation instructions
   - `aria-roledescription="Interactive maintenance scheduling canvas"`

2. **Created ARIA live region for announcements:**
   - Hidden div with `role="status"`, `aria-live="polite"`, `aria-atomic="true"`
   - Positioned off-screen with CSS (visually hidden but accessible)

3. **Added LOD change announcements:**
   - Tracks previous LOD level via ref
   - Announces when LOD changes: "View changed to [Strategic/Tactical/Detailed view description]"

4. **Added zoom level announcements:**
   - Debounced (500ms) to avoid spam during continuous zoom
   - Only announces significant changes (>10% difference)
   - Format: "X percent zoom"

5. **Added aria-labels to task elements in MicroLayer.tsx:**
   - Descriptive labels: "Maintenance task, Train X, Day Y, Duration Z days"
   - Includes conflict status if applicable

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (ARIA attributes, live region, announcements)
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx` (task element aria-labels)
- `features.json` (F022 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ App loads correctly at localhost:5175
- ✅ Accessibility tree shows `application` role with full aria-label
- ✅ `status` element visible in accessibility tree (live region)
- ✅ Canvas renders correctly with all features working

**Next Step:** All features complete! Project ready for review.

---

## Session: 2025-12-17 (F021 - Keyboard Navigation for Tasks) - COMPLETE ✅

### F021: Keyboard navigation for tasks - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Enable keyboard-based task navigation for accessibility. Users can Tab through tasks, use Arrow keys for directional navigation, and Enter/Space to select.

**Implementation:**

1. **Added focusedTaskId state to interaction.ts:**
   - New state field: `focusedTaskId: string | null`
   - Added reducers: `setFocusedTask()`, `clearFocus()`
   - State separate from selection (visual distinction)

2. **Added keyboard navigation helper functions to InfiniteCanvas.tsx:**
   - `getVisibleTasks()`: Filters tasks within viewport bounds, sorted by trainIndex then startDay
   - `findNextTaskInDirection()`: Finds nearest task in arrow key direction based on spatial proximity

3. **Updated handleKeyDown in InfiniteCanvas.tsx:**
   - **Tab**: Focus first/next visible task (cycles through visible tasks)
   - **Shift+Tab**: Focus previous visible task
   - **Enter/Space**: Select focused task (triggers Task Details panel via F026)
   - **Arrow keys** (when task focused): Navigate spatially to nearest task in direction
   - **Escape**: Clear focus

4. **Added focus visual indicators:**
   - **MesoLayer.tsx**:
     - Added FOCUS_COLOR constant (#8b5cf6 violet-500)
     - TaskBlocks component colors focused task purple
     - FocusRing component renders ring behind focused task
   - **MicroLayer.tsx**: Same pattern as MesoLayer

5. **Fixed viewportBounds hook ordering:**
   - Moved viewportBounds useMemo from line 1892 to line 731
   - Required because handleKeyDown uses viewportBounds in dependency array
   - Fixed "Cannot access 'viewportBounds' before initialization" error

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/state/interaction.ts` (focusedTaskId state)
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (keyboard handlers, helper functions, LODLayers focusedTaskId prop)
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx` (FocusRing, focus color)
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MicroLayer.tsx` (FocusRing, focus color)
- `features.json` (F021 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ App loads correctly at localhost:5175
- ✅ Tab key focuses first visible task (purple highlight visible on TRAIN-001)
- ✅ Enter key selects focused task (Task Details panel appears)
- ✅ Task Details shows correct info (Maintenance, TRAIN-001, Jan 6-8 2024)
- ✅ Console shows only pre-existing warnings (expandShaderIncludes from r3f-perf)

**Focus vs Selection Visual Design:**
- **Focus** (keyboard): Purple/violet (#8b5cf6) - distinct from selection
- **Selection** (click): Cyan (#06b6d4) - established in F019
- **Hover**: Brightened base color - established in F018

**Next Step:** F022 - ARIA labels for screen readers (now unblocked)

---

## Session: 2025-12-17 (Task Organization) - COMPLETE ✅

### Tarefas Organizadas e Status Atualizado

**Análise realizada:**
- Total de features: 59
- Features completas: 53 (passes: true)
- Features pendentes: 6 (passes: false)

**Correções feitas:**
1. **F123 marcada como COMPLETE** - Era redundante com F025 (Time scrubber minimap). Ambas tratavam do mesmo componente de minimap.

2. **Parallel execution atualizada** - Configuração anterior tinha features já completas (F023, F025, F017). Nova configuração:
   - Wave 1: F016, F026, F027+F028 (3 grupos paralelos)
   - Wave 2: F021 (navegação por teclado)

## Session: 2025-12-17 (Parallel Harness Setup) - COMPLETE ✅

### HARNESS-005: Parallel Execution Infrastructure - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Create infrastructure to run multiple features in parallel using git worktrees, enabling ~2.5x faster development.

**Research:**
- Studied [Anthropic's Effective Harnesses for Long-Running Agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- Researched git worktree patterns for AI agent parallel development
- Analyzed file conflict potential for all 10 pending features

**Analysis of Pending Features (10 total):**

```
WAVE 1 (Can run in parallel - 3 groups):
├── Group A: F023 (Dependency lines) → MicroLayer.tsx [LOW risk]
├── Group B: F123, F025 (Minimap) → App.tsx [MEDIUM risk, sequential]
└── Group C: F016, F017 (Scroll modifiers) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [HIGH risk, sequential]

WAVE 2 (After Wave 1 merge - 2 groups):
├── Group D: F027, F028 (Keyboard shortcuts) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [HIGH risk, sequential]
└── Group E: F026 (Task details panel) → App.tsx [MEDIUM risk]

WAVE 3 (After Wave 2 merge - 1 group):
└── Group F: F021 (Keyboard task navigation) → Multiple files [HIGH risk]

WAVE 4 (After Wave 3 merge - 1 group):
└── Group G: F022 (ARIA labels) → Multiple files [LOW risk]
```

**Files Created:**
- `scripts/parallel-config.ts` - Wave/group configuration with conflict analysis
- `scripts/parallel-runner.ts` - Interactive parallel execution runner
- `docs/PARALLEL_EXECUTION.md` - Complete documentation

**Files Modified:**
- `.gitignore` - Added `.trees/` for worktree directories
- `features.json` - Added `parallelExecution` section with waves configuration

**Key Features of Parallel Runner:**
1. **Git worktree management** - Creates isolated working directories
2. **Terminal session spawning** - Opens separate Claude sessions per feature
3. **Isolation prompts** - Each session restricted to its feature scope
4. **Wave dependencies** - Prevents starting Wave N before Wave N-1 completes
5. **Merge helper** - Automates feature branch merging
6. **Status display** - Shows progress across all waves/groups
7. **macOS notifications** - Alerts for session events
8. **Cleanup command** - Removes all worktrees

**Speedup Calculation:**
- Sequential: 10 features = 10 sessions
- Parallel: 4 waves (2+2+1+1) = ~4 "effective sessions"
- Estimated speedup: **2.5x**

**Usage:**
```bash
# Show status
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts status

# Execute Wave 1
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts wave 1

# Merge completed feature
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts merge F023
```

## Session: 2025-12-17 (TECH-DEBT-001 Implementation) - COMPLETE ✅

### TECH-DEBT-001: Migrate Camera State to @mms/toolkit State Machine - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Replace React useState/useRef with @mms/toolkit external store to solve stale closure problems in animation loops.

**Implementation:**

1. **Created viewport slice (`apps/canvas/src/state/viewport.ts`):**
   - ViewportSliceState interface with camera, canvasSize, momentum, animation, pan states
   - createSyncSlice with all reducers: setCamera, setMomentum, startAnimation, startPan, etc.
   - Cache policy: 'session' (resets when app closes)

2. **Created canvas machine (`apps/canvas/src/state/canvas-machine.ts`):**
   - createMachine with viewport slice
   - Exports: useCanvas, useCanvasSelector, canvasActions, canvasStore
   - Context with userID: null (anonymous canvas)

3. **Migrated InfiniteCanvas.tsx:**
   - Replaced useState calls with useCanvasSelector for reactive state
   - Animation loops use canvasStore.getState() for synchronous access (no stale closures)
   - Pan/zoom handlers use canvasStore.dispatch(canvasActions.viewport.xxx())
   - Removed cameraPosRef workaround (no longer needed with external store)

**Errors Fixed:**
- `cache: { strategy: 'none' }` → `cache: { policy: 'session' }` (toolkit requires 'policy' field)
- `context: {}` → `context: { userID: null }` (toolkit requires userID in context)

**Files Created:**
- `apps/canvas/src/state/viewport.ts` (~235 lines)
- `apps/canvas/src/state/canvas-machine.ts` (~55 lines)
- `apps/canvas/src/state/index.ts` (re-exports)

**Files Modified:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (full migration from useState to store)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ MACRO layer (0.44x): Heatmap renders correctly
- ✅ MESO layer (1.00x): Task blocks with row backgrounds
- ✅ MICRO layer (2.25x): Task cards with labels
- ✅ Zoom in/out buttons: Working (1.00x → 2.25x → 5.00x)
- ✅ LOD transitions: Smooth (MESO → MICRO → back)
- ✅ Reset View: Returns to initial state
- ✅ No console errors

**Benefits Achieved:**
- Animation loops always read current state via store.getState()
- No more stale closure issues in momentum/zoom animations
- Clean separation of concerns (state logic in slice, rendering in component)
- Foundation for future state slices (selection, hover, etc.)

## Session: 2025-12-16 (PERF-001 Implementation) - COMPLETE ✅

### PERF-001: Performance Monitor with Logging System - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Add real-time WebGL performance metrics tool with detailed logging for both visual and programmatic analysis.

**Implementation:**

1. **r3f-perf Integration:**
   - Installed r3f-perf 7.2.3 via `bun add r3f-perf`
   - Visual panel shows: GPU, CPU, FPS, draw calls, triangles, geometries, textures, shaders

2. **PerfMonitor Component (`apps/canvas/src/components/PerfMonitor.tsx`):**
   - ~400 lines of code with types, logging system, and visual panel
   - PerfLogger sub-component tracks metrics every frame
   - Circular buffer stores last 60s of performance data
   - Rolling averages calculated over 60-frame windows

3. **Global Console API:**
   - `window.__PERF_LOG__` - Access log buffer (last 60s)
   - `window.__PERF_EXPORT__()` - Export full log as array
   - `window.__PERF_START_SESSION__(name)` - Start recording session
   - `window.__PERF_STOP_SESSION__()` - Stop and get aggregated stats
   - `window.__PERF_SESSION_STATUS__()` - Check active session
   - `window.__PERF_CLEAR__()` - Clear log buffer

4. **Session Statistics:**
   - FPS: avg, min, max, p1 (worst 1%), p99 (best 99%)
   - MS: avg, max frame time
   - Frame counts: below 30fps, below 60fps
   - Duration and total frames

5. **UI Integration:**
   - Toggle visibility via 'P' key
   - "Perf" button in navbar
   - Panel positioned top-right
   - Runs in development mode by default

**Known Issue:**
- r3f-perf causes console spam with `expandShaderIncludes TypeError`
- Root cause: troika-three-utils 0.49.0 incompatible with Three.js 0.170+
- Fix exists in troika-three-utils 0.50.3+ but r3f-perf hasn't updated
- Tracked in: https://github.com/utsuboco/r3f-perf/issues/58
- Does NOT affect functionality - panel and logging work correctly

**Files Created/Modified:**
- `apps/canvas/src/components/PerfMonitor.tsx` (NEW)
- `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` (perf state, toggle, event tracking)
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (PerfMonitor props)
- `docs/PERFORMANCE_LOGGING.md` (NEW - comprehensive documentation)
- `features.json` (PERF-001 passes: true)

- Visual panel renders correctly (verified via Playwright MCP)
- Session API works: START_SESSION, SESSION_STATUS, STOP_SESSION
- Performance logging active even when panel hidden

## Session: 2025-12-16 (F124 - Sidebar/Timeline Navigation) - COMPLETE ✅

### F124: Sidebar and Timeline interactive navigation - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Enable interactive navigation via sidebar (train list) and timeline header. Users can click items to jump directly to specific trains/dates.

**Implementation:**

1. **InfiniteCanvas Navigation API:**
   - Added `NavigationTarget` interface with trainIndex, dayOffset, optional zoom
   - Added `navigationTarget` and `onNavigationComplete` props
   - Navigation handler calculates target camera position and animates (350ms)
   - Uses existing `animateZoomTo()` function for smooth transitions

2. **Sidebar Interactive Train List:**
   - Train IDs now clickable with `cursor: pointer`
   - Hover effect: background changes to `#e1e8ed`
   - Tooltip: "Click to navigate to TRAIN-XXX"
   - Click navigates canvas to center on selected train

3. **Timeline Interactive Date Labels:**
   - Date labels now clickable with `cursor: pointer`
   - Hover effect: background highlight + darker text color
   - Tooltip: "Click to navigate to [date]"
   - Click navigates canvas to center on selected date

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (NavigationTarget, navigation handler)
- `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` (navigation state, handlers, sidebar onClick)
- `apps/canvas/src/components/TimelineHeader.tsx` (onDateClick prop, label interactivity)
- `features.json` (F124 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ Sidebar shows "Click to navigate to TRAIN-XXX" tooltips
- ✅ Timeline shows "Click to navigate to [date]" tooltips
- ✅ Click TRAIN-040 → canvas centers on TRAIN-040 row
- ✅ Click Mar 2024 → canvas centers on March 2024
- ✅ Hover effects work (background highlight)
- ✅ No console errors

**Deferred to Future:**
- Search/filter functionality for quick navigation
- Keyboard shortcut (Cmd+G/Ctrl+G) for "Go to..."
- Autocomplete dropdown for direct navigation

**Next:** F123 (Minimap navigation), HARNESS-002 (reduce file sizes)

## Session: 2025-12-16 (F116 - Performance Optimization) - COMPLETE ✅

### F116: Performance optimization (60fps) - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Ensure 60fps performance during pan/zoom with InstancedMesh optimizations and frustum culling.

**Analysis:**
Reviewed all three LOD layers to identify performance bottlenecks:

1. **MesoLayer (BEFORE):**
   - TaskBlocks: InstancedMesh ✅ (already optimized)
   - ConflictGlows: InstancedMesh ✅ (already optimized)
   - RowBackgrounds: **50 individual meshes = 50 draw calls** ❌

2. **MicroLayer:** Already fully optimized
   - Frustum culling (filterVisibleTasks)
   - InstancedMesh for grid lines (2 draw calls)
   - InstancedMesh for row backgrounds
   - Lazy label rendering (max 50 labels)
   - Performance skip when opacity < 0.3

3. **MacroLayer:** Already optimized
   - InstancedMesh for heatmap cells
   - YearMarkers: ~11 individual meshes (acceptable)

**Solution:**
Converted MesoLayer RowBackgrounds from individual meshes to InstancedMesh:
- **Before:** 50 individual `<mesh>` elements = 50 draw calls
- **After:** 1 `<instancedMesh>` = 1 draw call
- **Improvement:** 98% reduction in draw calls for row backgrounds

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/MesoLayer.tsx` (RowBackgrounds using InstancedMesh)
- `features.json` (F116 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ MACRO view (0.44x): Heatmap renders correctly with color gradient
- ✅ MESO view (1.00x): Task blocks with alternating row backgrounds
- ✅ MICRO view (2.25x): Task cards with labels, grid lines visible
- ✅ LOD transitions smooth (no flickering)
- ✅ No console errors

**Performance Summary:**

| Layer | Component | Before | After |
|-------|-----------|--------|-------|
| MesoLayer | RowBackgrounds | 50 draw calls | 1 draw call |
| MesoLayer | TaskBlocks | 1 draw call | 1 draw call |
| MesoLayer | ConflictGlows | 1 draw call | 1 draw call |
| MicroLayer | All | Already optimized | - |
| MacroLayer | All | Already optimized | - |

**Next:** F123 (Minimap), F124 (Sidebar/Timeline navigation)

## Session: 2025-12-16 (F122 - Fix elastic/jump drag bug) - COMPLETE ✅

### F122: Fix elastic/jump effect when dragging - COMPLETE

**Problem:**
Canvas felt elastic or jumped when starting a new drag during/after momentum animation.

**Root Cause Analysis:**
React state updates are batched/async. When user starts a new drag while momentum is running:
1. `handlePointerDown` captures `cameraPos` from React state
2. But React state hasn't been updated yet with latest momentum position
3. New drag starts from stale position → canvas "jumps" back

**Solution Implemented:**
1. Added `cameraPosRef` to track camera position synchronously via useRef
2. Created `updateCameraPos()` helper that updates BOTH ref and state
3. Updated all `setCameraPos()` calls to use `updateCameraPos()`:
   - `handleCameraInitialized`
   - `animateZoomTo` (zoom animation loop)
   - `startMomentum` (momentum animation loop)
   - `handlePinchZoom`
   - `handlePointerMove` (pan movement)
   - `handleDoubleClick` (double-click zoom animation)
4. Changed `handlePointerDown` to read from `cameraPosRef.current` instead of `cameraPos` state

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (cameraPosRef, updateCameraPos helper)
- `features.json` (F122 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ Single drag pans correctly
- ✅ Drag during momentum - no jump/elastic effect
- ✅ Rapid drag-release-drag sequences (5 iterations) - smooth panning
- ✅ No console errors

### Harness Updates

**F117 Archived:**
- "Full visual parity with POC reference" no longer relevant
- Production app has surpassed POC in functionality and UX
- Moved to archive.deferredFeatures

**F124 Added (Feature Request):**
- Sidebar and Timeline interactive navigation
- Click train IDs to navigate directly
- Click dates in timeline to navigate
- Search/filter for quick navigation
- Research best UX patterns (Figma, VS Code, design tools)

**Next:** F124 (Sidebar/Timeline navigation), F123 (Minimap), F116 (Performance)

---

## Session: 2025-12-16 (F120 + F121 - Double-click zoom + Scroll normalization) - COMPLETE ✅

### F120: Double-click to zoom in - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Implement double-click to zoom in 2x centered on click location.

**Implementation:**
- Already implemented in F119 (smooth zoom) session
- `handleDoubleClick` function in InfiniteCanvas.tsx (lines 1009-1074)
- Zooms 2x toward click point with 350ms cubicOut animation
- Respects MAX_ZOOM (5.0x) limit

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ Double-click at 1.00x → 2.00x (2x zoom)
- ✅ Double-click at 2.00x → 4.00x (2x zoom)
- ✅ Double-click at 4.00x → 5.00x (clamped to MAX_ZOOM)
- ✅ Double-click at 5.00x stays at 5.00x (respects limit)
- ✅ Smooth animation with cubicOut easing
- ✅ No console errors

### F121: Cross-platform scroll/input normalization - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Normalize scroll sensitivity across macOS/Windows/Linux and trackpad/mouse.

**Implementation:**

1. **deltaMode Normalization:**
   - LINE mode (deltaMode=1): multiply by LINE_HEIGHT (40px)
   - PAGE mode (deltaMode=2): multiply by PAGE_HEIGHT (800px)
   - PIXEL mode (deltaMode=0): use directly

2. **Trackpad vs Mouse Detection:**
   - Track event frequency with timestamps
   - Events < 20ms apart = trackpad behavior
   - After 3+ rapid events, mark as trackpad
   - Large deltas (>50px) reset to mouse mode

3. **Adaptive Sensitivity:**
   - Mouse: MOUSE_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.003
   - Trackpad: TRACKPAD_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001 (lower for many small events)

4. **Zoom Delta Clamping:**
   - MAX_ZOOM_DELTA_PER_EVENT = 0.5
   - Prevents jump zoom from large scroll events

**Constants Added:**
```typescript
LINE_HEIGHT = 40                    // Pixels per line
PAGE_HEIGHT = 800                   // Pixels per page
MOUSE_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.003      // Base zoom sensitivity
TRACKPAD_ZOOM_SENSITIVITY = 0.001   // Lower for trackpad
MAX_ZOOM_DELTA_PER_EVENT = 0.5      // Cap per event
TRACKPAD_DETECTION_THRESHOLD = 20   // ms between events
TRACKPAD_EVENT_COUNT_THRESHOLD = 3  // Rapid events to confirm
```

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (normalizeWheelDelta function, updated handleWheel)
- `features.json` (F120, F121 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ Scroll down (deltaY=300) zooms out: 1.00x → 0.61x
- ✅ Scroll up (deltaY=-500) zooms in: 0.61x → 1.00x
- ✅ No console errors
- ✅ Smooth zoom animation

**Next:** F116 (Performance), F117 (Visual parity)

---

## Session: 2025-12-16 (F118 - Momentum Panning) - COMPLETE ✅

### F118: Momentum/Inertia Panning - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Implement velocity-based drag with momentum/inertia like Google Maps and iOS.

**Implementation:**

1. **Velocity Tracking:**
   - Track pointer position samples during drag with timestamps
   - Store last N samples (5) for velocity calculation
   - Calculate total displacement and time to get velocity

2. **Momentum Animation:**
   - On pointer release, calculate average velocity from samples
   - Start `requestAnimationFrame` loop with initial velocity
   - Apply friction (0.93) per frame to gradually slow down
   - Stop when velocity < 0.5 world units

3. **Integration:**
   - Momentum stops on any user interaction (new drag, zoom)
   - Cleanup animation on component unmount
   - Camera position clamped to world bounds during animation

**Constants Used:**
```typescript
MOMENTUM_FRICTION = 0.93     // Per-frame velocity multiplier
MOMENTUM_MIN_VELOCITY = 0.5  // Stop threshold
MOMENTUM_VELOCITY_SAMPLES = 5 // Samples to average
```

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (momentum implementation)
- `features.json` (F118 passes: true)

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ App loads at Jan 2024 (initial position)
- ✅ Fast drag moves view with momentum continuation
- ✅ View moved from Jan 2024 to Dec 2024 with single fast drag
- ✅ Momentum respects world bounds
- ✅ No runtime errors related to momentum

Here are the search results for WebGL spatial indexing with RBush R-tree for viewport queries and performance:

## RBush Overview

RBush is a high-performance JavaScript library for 2D spatial indexing of points and rectangles. It's based on an optimized R-tree data structure with bulk insertion support.(no content)Spatial index is a special data structure for points and rectangles that allows you to perform queries like "all items within this bounding box" very efficiently (e.g. hundreds of times faster than looping over all items). It's most commonly used in maps and data visualizations.

## Performance Characteristics

**Bulk Loading:**
- Bulk insertion is usually ~2-3 times faster than inserting items one by one.
- After bulk loading (bulk insertion into an empty tree), subsequent query performance is also ~20-30% better.

**Query Performance:**
- RBush uses a bulk-loading algorithm for efficient insertion, making it faster than traditional R-trees when dealing with large datasets. Its performance is particularly notable in point and rectangle queries, where it can achieve logarithmic time complexity.

**Node Size Configuration:**
- An optional argument to RBush defines the maximum number of entries in a tree node. 9 (used by default) is a reasonable choice for most applications. Higher value means faster insertion and slower search, and vice versa.

## Use Cases

Rbush is ideal for applications that require real-time updates and fast querying, such as mapping applications, game development, and any scenario where spatial data changes frequently. Its efficiency in handling dynamic datasets makes it a go-to choice for developers needing quick access to spatial information.

## Alternatives

- If you're indexing a static list of points (you don't need to add/remove points after indexing), you should use kdbush which performs point indexing 5-8x faster than RBush.

- **Flatbush**: An efficient implementation of the packed Hilbert R-tree algorithm. Enables fast spatial queries on a very large number of objects (e.g. millions), which is very useful in maps, data visualizations and computational geometry algorithms.

## Server-Client Workflow

Importing and exporting as JSON allows you to use RBush on both the server (using Node.js) and the browser combined, e.g. first indexing the data on the server and then importing the resulting tree data on the client for searching.

- Questions - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/how-to-do-frustum-culling-with-instancedmesh/22633"},{"title":"VR Me Up - Three.js InstancedMesh Performance Optimizations","url":"https://vrmeup.com/devlog/devlog_10_threejs_instancedmesh_performance_optimizations.html"},{"title":"Problem implementing frustum culling and sorting for InstancedMesh · Issue #28102 · mrdoob/three.js","url":"https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/28102"},{"title":"@three.ez/instanced-mesh - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/@three.ez/instanced-mesh"},{"title":"InstancedMesh: Proposal to support sorting, frustum culling · Issue #27170 · mrdoob/three.js","url":"https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/issues/27170"},{"title":"Three.ez/InstancedMesh2 - Enhanced InstancedMesh with frustum culling, fast raycasting (BVH), sorting, visibility management, LOD, skinning and more - Resources - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/three-ez-instancedmesh2-enhanced-instancedmesh-with-frustum-culling-fast-raycasting-bvh-sorting-visibility-management-lod-skinning-and-more/69344"},{"title":"Three.ez/InstancedMesh2 - Enhanced InstancedMesh with frustum culling, fast raycasting (BVH), sorting, visibility management and more - Resources - three.js forum","url":"https://discourse.threejs.org/t/three-ez-instancedmesh2-enhanced-instancedmesh-with-frustum-culling-fast-raycasting-bvh-sorting-visibility-management-and-more/69344"},{"title":"Exciting New Features Coming to Three.js - What Developers Need to Know","url":"https://moldstud.com/articles/p-exciting-new-features-coming-to-threejs-what-developers-need-to-know"}]

Here are the search results for Three.js frustum culling with InstancedMesh performance optimization:

## Key Findings

### The Core Problem
Frustum culling for individual instances is a feature not supported natively by three.js.(no content)In native object mode, meshes are removed by the view frustum culling step in the Three.js render pipeline and never sent to the GPU. In instanced mode, however, all the meshes are sent to the GPU. Even though the GPU may not render the entire mesh, as it may be outside its triangle view frustum culling step, the mesh still has to go through the vertex shader.(no content)On devices with a low end GPU, this can actually decrease performance.

### Solution: InstancedMesh2 Library (`@three.ez/instanced-mesh`)
InstancedMesh2 is an alternative version of InstancedMesh with enhanced features for performance and usability. Key features include:

- Per-instance frustum culling: skip rendering for out-of-view instances.
- Object3D-like instances: use instances like Object3D with transforms and custom data.
- Dynamic capacity: add or remove instances seamlessly.

### BVH Optimization
Avoiding rendering objects outside the camera frustum can drastically improve performance (especially for complex geometries). Frustum culling by default is performed by iterating all instances, but it is possible to speed up this process by creating a spatial indexing data structure (BVH).(no content)To speed up raycasting and frustum culling, a spatial indexing data structure can be created to contain the boundingBoxes of all instances. This works very well if the instances are mostly static (updating a BVH can be expensive) and scattered in world space.

### Implementation Technique
To solve this, allocate space for a maximum number of instances when creating the Instanced Mesh but set the count to only render the number of instances that are visible. On each frame, iterate over the instances and move the visible ones to the front of the instance array and the ones outside the view frustum to the hidden section and reset the count.

### Performance Results
Using multiple InstancedMesh objects for each level of detail, combined with the View Frustum culling almost doubled the frame rate.

### Recent Discussion (July 2025)
The most straightforward way seems to be reordering the instances and all attribute buffers and then play with the 'count' property to draw the non-culled instances.

### Official Three.js Proposals
A library was developed that adds frustum culling to InstancedMesh, with plans to implement culling and sorting on InstancedMesh on the main repository.(no content)Similar to how the WebGL_multi_draw extension is used for BatchedMesh, there are "instanced" variants of the multidraw functions that allow for sorting the order in which instanced draws are performed while retaining a single draw call. If desired, InstancedMesh could switch to use the multidraw extension and provide support for sorting objects within a single InstancedMesh.

To solve this, allocate space for a maximum number of instances when creating the Instanced Mesh but set the count to only render the number of instances that are visible. On each frame, iterate over the instances and move the visible ones to the front of the instance array and the ones outside the view frustum to the hidden section and reset the count.

### Performance Results
Using multiple InstancedMesh objects for each level of detail, combined with the View Frustum culling almost doubled the frame rate.

### Recent Discussion (July 2025)
The most straightforward way seems to be reordering the instances and all attribute buffers and then play with the 'count' property to draw the non-culled instances.

### Official Three.js Proposals
A library was developed that adds frustum culling to InstancedMesh, with plans to implement culling and sorting on InstancedMesh on the main repository.(no content)Similar to how the WebGL_multi_draw extension is used for BatchedMesh, there are "instanced" variants of the multidraw functions that allow for sorting the order in which instanced draws are performed while retaining a single draw call. If desired, InstancedMesh could switch to use the multidraw extension and provide support for sorting objects within a single InstancedMesh.

Here are the search results for React Three Fiber (R3F) viewport culling and large dataset performance in 2025:

## Official R3F Performance Documentation

Running WebGL can be quite expensive depending on how powerful your devices are. In order to mitigate this, especially if you want to make your application available to a broad variety of devices, including weaker options, you should look into performance optimizations.(no content)Three.js apps usually run in a game-loop that executes 60 times a second, React Three Fiber is no different. This is perfectly fine when your scene has constantly moving parts in it. This is what generally drains batteries the most and makes fans spin up. But if the moving parts in your scene are allowed to come to rest, then it would be wasteful to keep rendering. In such cases you can opt into on-demand rendering, which will only render when necessary.

## Key Performance Pitfalls

The most important gotcha in three.js is that creating objects can be expensive, think twice before you mount/unmount things! Every material or light that you put into the scene has to compile, every geometry you create will be processed.(no content)Try to use instancing as much as you can when you need to display many objects of a similar type!(no content)Threejs has a render-loop, it does not work like the DOM does. Fast updates are carried out in useFrame by mutation. useFrame is your per-component render-loop.

## 2025 Optimization Guide (Codrops - February 2025)

r3f-perf for react-three-fiber displays statistics on shaders, textures, and the number of vertices.(no content)Not all objects in a scene require the same level of detail. Distant objects can have simplified models and low-resolution textures without significantly impacting the visual experience. Therefore, it is crucial to first understand how objects will be positioned relative to the camera.

## Performance Monitoring Tools

- r3f-perf allows you to easily monitor the performances of your @react-three/fiber application. Add the Perf component anywhere in your Canvas.

## Known Issues with Large Datasets

Some users have experienced significant performance drops when using R3F/drei Instances compared to vanilla JavaScript for rendering multiple instances of BoxGeometry.(no content)When generating multiple instances of BoxGeometry directly in JavaScript (or in pure R3F), users get high frame rates and no performance issues. However, when implementing the identical code as a component using R3F/drei, they observe a drastic FPS drop with high CPU usage.

---

**Note:** The search didn't return specific results about "viewport culling" techniques for R3F in 2025. You may want to look into:
- Three.js built-in frustum culling (`mesh.frustumCulled = true`)
- Level of Detail (LOD) implementations
- Spatial data structures like octrees for large datasets
- The `@react-three/drei` library's `<Detailed>` component for LOD

Links: [{"title":"deck.gl/dev-docs/RFCs/proposals/layer-culling-rfc.md at master · visgl/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/blob/master/dev-docs/RFCs/proposals/layer-culling-rfc.md"},{"title":"Viewport | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/viewport"},{"title":"Performance Optimization | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/performance"},{"title":"deck.gl/bindings/pydeck/examples/04 - Plotting massive data sets.ipynb at master · visgl/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/blob/master/bindings/pydeck/examples/04 - Plotting massive data sets.ipynb"},{"title":"deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/performance.md at master · visgl/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/blob/master/docs/developer-guide/performance.md"},{"title":"Views and Projections | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/views"},{"title":"Point cloud layer performance optimization · visgl/deck.gl · Discussion #7454","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/discussions/7454"},{"title":"GPU Accelerated Aggregation in deck.gl | by Ravi Akkenapally | vis.gl | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/vis-gl/gpu-accelerated-aggregation-in-deck-gl-7e2c7d701fb0"},{"title":"Rendering A Minecraft World With deck.gl | by vis.gl | vis.gl | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/vis-gl/rendering-a-minecraft-world-with-deck-gl-927b52c5c6db"},{"title":"Deck | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/deck"}]

Here are the search results for deck.gl massive data rendering with 1 million points and viewport culling techniques:

## Performance Benchmarks

On 2015 MacBook Pros with dual graphics cards, most basic layers (like ScatterplotLayer) render fluidly at 60 FPS during pan and zoom operations up to about 1M (one million) data items, with framerates dropping into low double digits (10-20FPS) when the data sets approach 10M items.(no content)Even if interactivity is not an issue, browser limitations on how big chunks of contiguous memory can be allocated (e.g., Chrome caps individual allocations at 1GB) will cause most layers to crash during GPU buffer generation somewhere between 10M and 100M items. You would need to break up your data into chunks and use multiple deck.gl layers to get past this limit.

## Frustum/Viewport Culling

Frustum culling for 3D tiles is proposed to be generalized so that all deck.gl layers can benefit from this standard 3D engine feature. The ability to avoid rendering non-visible geometries is particularly important for large segmented data sets such as 3D Tile and potree point clouds, where hundreds of millions of points can be loaded into memory across hundreds of tiles.(no content)When moving in first-person view inside big point clouds, the idea is to only render things within the current view frustum. This can lead to substantial performance increase, often 3-4x depending on direction of view and how tight the view frustum is, which can turn 5 FPS into 20 FPS.(no content)Culling requires intersecting bounding volumes with the view frustum, using a range of well known but not completely trivial 3D geometry techniques.

## Performance Optimization Techniques

### Data Chunking
When the data prop changes, the layer will recalculate all of its GPU buffers. The time required for this is proportional to the number of items in your data prop. This step is the most expensive operation that a layer does - also on CPU - potentially affecting the responsiveness of the application. It may take multiple seconds for multi-million item layers.

### Fragment Overdraw
Excessive overdraw (drawing many objects/pixels on top of each other) can generate very high fragment counts and hurt performance. A Scatterplot radius of 5 pixels generates ~100 pixels per point. If you have a Scatterplot layer with 10 million points, this can result in up to 1 billion fragment shader invocations per frame. While dependent on zoom levels (clipping will improve performance to some extent), this many fragments will certainly strain even a recent MacBook Pro GPU.

### GPU Aggregation
When input data contains millions of points, aggregating them in JavaScript puts a heavy load on the CPU. Moreover, the aggregation process may need to be repeated frequently. However, computations involved in aggregation can be executed in parallel, and hence are very suitable to perform on GPU. When aggregation is performed on GPU, we can aggregate millions of points with no performance hits and resulting user experience is much smoother.(no content)Up to 12X speedups were observed when aggregation is accelerated using GPU for ScreenGridLayer. An example of aggregating 4.5 million points showed very responsive UI with GPU acceleration, but unresponsive UI when performing the same aggregation on CPU.

### Layer Filtering
The `layerFilter` callback, if supplied, will be called before a layer is drawn to determine whether it should be rendered. This gives the application an opportunity to filter out layers from the layer list during either rendering or picking. Filtering can be done per viewport or per layer or both.

content.content:
1→# Railway Rolling Stock Maintenance Scheduling: Research & Industry Practices
     2→
     3→> Comprehensive documentation for RAG system - Dubai Metro MMS Project
     4→> Last updated: December 2025
     5→
     6→## Table of Contents
     7→
     8→1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
     9→2. [Problem Definition](#problem-definition)
    10→3. [Planning Hierarchy](#planning-hierarchy)
    11→4. [Optimization Techniques](#optimization-techniques)
    12→5. [Physical Constraints Modeling](#physical-constraints-modeling)
    13→6. [Case Studies by Metro System](#case-studies-by-metro-system)
    14→7. [Technology & Systems](#technology--systems)
    15→8. [Data Architecture](#data-architecture)
    16→9. [Academic Research](#academic-research)
    17→10. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
    18→11. [Risk Management & Edge Cases](#risk-management--edge-cases)
    19→12. [Human-in-the-Loop Integration](#human-in-the-loop-integration)
    20→13. [Change Propagation & Recalculation](#change-propagation--recalculation-mechanics)
    21→14. [Future Directions](#future-directions)
    22→15. [References](#references)
    23→16. [Glossary](#appendix-glossary)
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Executive Summary
    28→
    29→Railway rolling stock maintenance scheduling is a complex optimization problem that combines:
    30→- **Resource allocation** (man-hours, tracks, depots)
    31→- **Time/mileage constraints** (preventive maintenance intervals)
    32→- **Operational requirements** (train availability, service levels)
    33→- **Cost optimization** (labor, materials, penalties)
    34→
    35→Key findings from our research:
    36→
    37→| Aspect | Industry Standard |
    38→|--------|------------------|
    39→| Planning horizons | Daily → Weekly → Monthly → Annual → Long-term (10+ years) |
    40→| Primary trigger | Mileage-based (evolving to condition-based) |
    41→| Common techniques | MIP, Genetic Algorithms, Large Neighborhood Search, Hybrid CP/MIP |
    42→| Software platforms | IBM Maximo, SAP EAM, Custom optimization engines |
    43→| Performance target | 99.9% train availability (Hong Kong MTR benchmark) |
    44→
    45→---
    46→
    47→## Problem Definition
    48→
    49→### The Core Scheduling Problem
    50→
    51→The railway maintenance scheduling problem involves:
    52→
    53→1. **Fleet Management**: Multiple train types with different maintenance requirements
    54→2. **Maintenance Types**: Varying intervals and durations
    55→   - Daily inspections
    56→   - Monthly checks (~15,000 km)
    57→   - Quarterly services (~45,000 km)
    58→   - Annual overhauls (~180,000 km)
    59→   - Major overhauls (4-6 years)
    60→
    61→3. **Resource Constraints**:
    62→   - Limited maintenance tracks
    63→   - Fixed workforce (man-hours per period)
    64→   - Depot capacity
    65→   - Spare parts availability
    66→
    67→4. **Objectives** (often conflicting):
    68→   - Minimize maintenance costs
    69→   - Maximize train availability
    70→   - Level resource utilization
    71→   - Comply with safety regulations
    72→
    73→### Problem Complexity
    74→
    75→According to research from [Wiley - Optimization of Annual Planned Rail Maintenance](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
    76→
    77→> "Using the CPLEX MIP solver, researchers managed to solve only 62% of instances within 3 hours. Kiefer et al. (2018) formulated their problem as an MIP model but quickly found runtime limitations on larger instances, exceeding the 24-hour time limit."
    78→
    79→This demonstrates why heuristic and metaheuristic approaches are essential for real-world applications.
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## Planning Hierarchy
    84→
    85→### Multi-Level Planning Structure
    86→
    87→Based on [JICA Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf) and industry practices:
    88→
    89→```
    90→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    91→│                     MASTER PLAN (Annual/Multi-Year)             │
    92→│  - Resource allocation across the contract period               │
    93→│  - Major overhaul scheduling                                    │
    94→│  - Budget planning                                              │
    95→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    96→│                     MONTHLY PLAN                                │
    97→│  - Medium-scale repairs allocation                              │
    98→│  - Workload balancing                                           │
    99→│  - Resource adjustment                                          │
   100→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   101→│                     WEEKLY PLAN                                 │
   102→│  - Detailed work orders                                         │
   103→│  - Staff rostering                                              │
   104→│  - Track allocation                                             │
   105→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   106→│                     DAILY PLAN                                  │
   107→│  - Execution scheduling                                         │
   108→│  - Real-time adjustments                                        │
   109→│  - Emergency response                                           │
   110→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   111→```
   112→
   113→### Tokyo Metro Approach
   114→
   115→From [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf):
   116→
   117→> "Tokyo Metro employs an 'Equalizing' approach to secure the required work progress, equalize the maintenance workload, and enable rolling stock maintenance personnel to fulfill their duties."
   118→
   119→Key principles:
   120→- **Even workload distribution** across periods
   121→- **Mileage tracking** for devices requiring km-based maintenance
   122→- **Train allocation** at daily, monthly, and annual levels
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## Optimization Techniques
   127→
   128→### 1. Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
   129→
   130→**Strengths:**
   131→- Mathematically optimal solutions
   132→- Well-understood theory
   133→- Commercial solvers available (Gurobi, CPLEX)
   134→
   135→**Limitations:**
   136→- Scalability issues with large instances
   137→- Long computation times
   138→- Requires linearization of constraints
   139→
   140→**Example Application - Deutsche Bahn:**
   141→
   142→From [Deutsche Bahn Hypergraph Optimization Case Study](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069):
   143→
   144→> "DB attributes annual savings of €74 million, an annual reduction of 34,000 tons of CO2 emissions, and the elimination of 600 coupling operations to the implementation of FEO (Fleet Employment Optimization)."
   145→
   146→### 2. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
   147→
   148→**Strengths:**
   149→- Handles complex, non-linear constraints
   150→- Good for multi-objective optimization
   151→- Scalable to large problems
   152→
   153→**Example Application - Hong Kong MTR:**
   154→
   155→From [CityU AI Projects - MTR Scheduling](https://www.cs.cityu.edu.hk/~hwchun/AIProjects/stories/allocation/mtrcscheduling/):
   156→
   157→> "The scheduling system is based on genetic algorithm and heuristic search, which work together with a rule-based system. The AI system improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, contributing to MTR's 99.9% on-time performance for 5 million daily passengers."
   158→
   159→### 3. Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)
   160→
   161→**Strengths:**
   162→- Effective for large-scale problems
   163→- Combines local search with diversification
   164→- Can escape local optima
   165→
   166→**Example Application - Vienna Tram:**
   167→
   168→From [ScienceDirect - Integrated Railway Planning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340):
   169→
   170→> "Kiefer et al. developed a metaheuristic based on large neighborhood search after MIP exceeded 24-hour time limits. Testing on the Vienna tram network—the largest instance in literature—showed effective results."
   171→
   172→### 4. Hybrid Constraint Programming / MIP
   173→
   174→**Strengths:**
   175→- CP generates feasible solutions quickly
   176→- MIP improves solution quality
   177→- Best of both worlds
   178→
   179→**Example Application - Danish Railways:**
   180→
   181→From [ScienceDirect - Hybrid CP/MIP Framework](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646):
   182→
   183→> "Whereas using a general purpose MIP solver is only able to solve instances over a two-week planning horizon, the hybrid framework generates good results for problem instances over an eight-week period."
   184→
   185→### 5. Greedy Metaheuristics
   186→
   187→**Strengths:**
   188→- Fast computation
   189→- Good initial solutions
   190→- Easy to implement
   191→
   192→**Example Application - Netherlands ProRail:**
   193→
   194→From [Wiley - Annual Rail Maintenance Optimization](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
   195→
   196→> "A hybrid algorithm using evolution strategy and a greedy algorithm was created and shown to outperform all other algorithms, achieving a two-thirds decrease in costs compared to human scheduler solutions."
   197→
   198→### Technique Comparison Matrix
   199→
   200→| Technique | Problem Size | Solution Quality | Speed | Implementation |
   201→|-----------|--------------|------------------|-------|----------------|
   202→| MIP | Small-Medium | Optimal | Slow | Complex |
   203→| Genetic Algorithm | Large | Good | Medium | Moderate |
   204→| Large Neighborhood Search | Large | Good | Fast | Moderate |
   205→| Hybrid CP/MIP | Medium-Large | Very Good | Medium | Complex |
   206→| Greedy Heuristics | Any | Acceptable | Very Fast | Simple |
   207→
   208→### Algorithm Selection Strategy for MMS
   209→
   210→Based on the research, a layered algorithmic architecture is recommended:
   211→
   212→1. **Strategic/Long-Term (Annual):** MIP for capacity planning and yearly workload leveling
   213→2. **Tactical/Medium-Term (Weeks):** CP or MIP with decomposition (LBBD) for slot assignment respecting depot constraints
   214→3. **Operational/Real-Time (Hours):** Heuristics and Metaheuristics for fast rescheduling during disruptions
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## Physical Constraints Modeling
   219→
   220→A common failure in traditional planning systems is over-abstraction of physical reality. The MMS must rigorously model depot and rolling stock constraints to ensure generated plans are executable.
   221→
   222→### Pit Occupancy and Finite Capacity
   223→
   224→Depot capacity is not just a track count; it's a complex resource and geometry constraint problem.
   225→
   226→**The "Tetris" Spatial Problem:**
   227→Trains have different lengths (4-car, 8-car). Pits have fixed lengths. A "Pit Occupancy" constraint must model spatial arrangement:
   228→- Can two 4-car trains fit in an 8-car pit?
   229→- What order must they enter (LIFO - Last-In-First-Out)?
   230→
   231→This requires modeling tracks not just as "resources" but as "capacitated resources" with linear spatial dimensions.
   232→
   233→**Finite Capacity Modeling:**
   234→```
   235→∑ Occupancy_t ≤ Capacity_t  (for all resource types simultaneously)
   236→```
   237→
   238→**Deadlock Prevention:**
   239→In dense depots, one train's movement can block another. "Deadlocks" occur when circular movement dependencies prevent any train from moving.
   240→
   241→The MMS must incorporate deadlock prevention algorithms (such as adaptations of the Banker's Algorithm or graph cycle detection) during shunting planning to ensure every scheduled movement sequence is geometrically viable.
   242→
   243→### Shunting Time and Dynamics
   244→
   245→The time to move a train from parking track to maintenance pit is non-zero and variable.
   246→
   247→**Train Unit Shunting Problem (TUSP):**
   248→This involves matching arriving train units with departing services and parking them in LIFO stacks on depot tracks. Stack order is critical; a train departing at 05:00 cannot be blocked by one leaving at 08:00.
   249→
   250→**Crossover Conflicts:**
   251→Modeling must account for conflicts where a shunting movement crosses a switch that blocks other movements. Constraints must impose temporal separation:
   252→```
   253→If Train A uses Switch S at time t,
   254→Train B cannot use Switch S until t + clearance_time
   255→```
   256→
   257→**Shunting Duration:**
   258→The system cannot assume instantaneous movement. Shunting times must be modeled based on track topology and speed limits (typically 5 km/h in yards).
   259→
   260→### Possession Windows and Infrastructure Availability
   261→
   262→Rolling stock maintenance often competes with infrastructure maintenance (permanent way, signaling, catenary).
   263→
   264→**Possession Windows:**
   265→Constraints must align rolling stock movements with possession windows—periods when tracks are closed for maintenance. This requires a unified data model where infrastructure availability acts as a hard constraint on fleet movement.
   266→
